Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Johann Friedrich Herbart

1776 – 1841

Mathematical psychology and the dynamics of competing representations.

Herbart’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a deliberately anti-faculty, anti-idealist, mathematically ambitious, and metaphysically realist theory of mental life. He rejects the idea that mind is explained by innate faculties such as memory, imagination, understanding, reason, desire, and will. Those labels classify phenomena; they do not explain them. What really explains mental life, for Herbart, is the lawful interaction of Vorstellungen—usually translated as “presentations,” “representations,” or “ideas”—which arise as the soul’s acts of self-preservation against disturbances. Consciousness is not a special inner theater presided over by an ego; it is the current field of effective presentations above a threshold. Memory, attention, perception, desire, affect, will, and self-consciousness are different configurations, motions, fusions, inhibitions, and reproductions of presentations.

Yet this is not materialism. Herbart grounds psychology in a simple, non-extended, incorporeal soul whose own nature is unknowable and which has no innate thoughts, feelings, desires, faculties, spatial parts, or temporal properties. His view is therefore a peculiar hybrid: a non-Cartesian soul-metaphysics combined with a proto-naturalistic dynamics of mental representation. The “ego” or “I” is not identical with the metaphysical soul. It is a formed, empirical, apperceptive complex: a self-representing system of presentations organized around body, memory, action, social recognition, and moral character. This distinction between soul, mind, consciousness, and ego is the core of Herbart’s philosophy of mind.

Historically, Herbart stands at a crucial transition point between early modern rational psychology and later scientific psychology. He accepts enough of Kant to reject intellectual intuition and the self-transparent metaphysical ego, but he rejects Kant’s pessimism about psychology as an exact science. He anticipates later ideas of dynamic unconscious processing, attentional threshold, top-down assimilation, self as constructed, and formal modeling of cognition. His mathematical details were widely criticized and did not become the basis of modern psychology, but his conceptual innovations helped shape nineteenth-century psychology, pedagogy, psychoanalysis, phenomenology, and debates about whether the mind can be treated scientifically. The Stanford Encyclopedia describes him as a founding figure of modern psychology and educational theory and notes that his limen, subconscious, apperception, anti-faculty psychology, and representational dynamics influenced later figures including Fechner, Wundt, Freud, Brentano, and Husserl.

1. Texts, terminology, and scope

The central psychological works are Lehrbuch zur Psychologie—first published in 1816, revised in 1834, and translated into English as A Text-book in Psychology—and the larger Psychologie als Wissenschaft, neu gegründet auf Erfahrung, Metaphysik und Mathematik of 1824–1825. The English translation’s subtitle captures Herbart’s ambition: to found psychology “on experience, metaphysics, and mathematics.”

The most important term is Vorstellung. In older English Herbartian literature it is often rendered “concept” or “idea”; recent scholarship often prefers “presentation,” because the term is broader than a logical concept and more active than a mere mental image. A Vorstellung can be a sensory presentation, memory-content, representational tendency, imagined object, conceptual content, or a component of desire. Herbart’s psychology is a mechanics of Vorstellungen, not merely an association of ideas in the Humean sense. Huemer and Landerer note that Vorstellung is hard to translate and that “presentation” is often used to avoid the misleading narrowness of “idea” or “concept.”

A second terminological difficulty is “soul.” Herbart uses Seele, Psyche, Geist, and Gemüt in related but not identical ways. The “soul” is the metaphysical simple real that grounds mental life. Geist is the soul considered as representing. Gemüt concerns the soul’s affective and conative life—feeling, desire, mood, inclination—but Herbart insists that these are not separate faculties. They are states or movements of presentations.

2. Historical problem: after Kant, against faculty psychology and idealism

Herbart inherits a Kantian problem. Kant had denied that empirical psychology could become an exact natural science in the same way as physics, partly because inner phenomena lack spatial measurability and partly because introspection alters the phenomena it tries to observe. Landerer and Huemer summarize this Kantian background: mental phenomena, on Kant’s view, have no spatial dimension, which makes mathematization of psychology impossible.

Herbart’s response is radical. He tries to do precisely what Kant thought could not be done: construct a lawful, quasi-physical, mathematical psychology. But he does not do so by reducing mind to brain. He begins from metaphysics, identifies presentations as the elementary psychological units, and then treats their intensities, conflicts, fusions, and reproductions as calculable magnitudes. In his Text-book, he states that the “concepts”—that is, presentations—must be regarded as forces whose effectiveness depends on strength, opposition, and combination.

His immediate polemical target is faculty psychology. In faculty psychology, mental life is explained by positing powers such as memory, imagination, understanding, reason, feeling, and will. Herbart objects that these are merely class names. To say that I remember because I have a memory faculty is not an explanation; it is a redescription. The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes his objection: faculties are class concepts, not causal powers, and the theory fails to describe the particular mental processes that a genuine empirical science must explain.

This anti-faculty stance also places him against parts of Kant and German Idealism. Against Kant, he denies innate forms and faculties as psychological explanations. Against Fichte, he rejects the self-positing ego as the ground of consciousness. Against Hegelian idealism, he insists on plural, mind-independent reals and on psychology as a disciplined science rather than speculative dialectic. Herbart was Kant’s second successor at Königsberg in 1809, but his philosophy moved in a sharply different direction.

3. Method: experience, metaphysics, mathematics

Herbart’s psychology is founded on three elements.

First, it begins from experience. The phenomena to be explained are actual mental states: perceiving, remembering, desiring, attending, judging, acting, and becoming self-conscious. Herbart does not deny empirical psychology; he thinks empirical psychology is indispensable.

Second, psychology requires metaphysics. Experience alone gives a fluctuating manifold of states; it does not by itself give the explanatory ground of their unity. Herbart’s metaphysics supplies the postulate of a simple soul and the general doctrine of self-preservation. In his method of relations, apparent contradictions—such as one self with many thoughts, or one thing with many properties—are resolved by analyzing the relations that make plurality and unity intelligible together. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that Herbart treats both the “thing” and the “ego” as problems of unity amid plurality and uses the method of relations to avoid treating either unity or multiplicity as simply self-standing.

Third, psychology requires mathematics. Herbart believes presentations differ by degree: strength, vividness, inhibition, connection, and opposition. If they differ by degree, they can in principle be submitted to mathematical treatment. The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes the view: representations must be treated as forces whose effectiveness depends on strength, oppositions, and connections, all of which differ in degree.

His psychology is therefore neither introspective common sense nor pure metaphysics. It is a speculative-explanatory science. He criticizes introspection because intentional self-observation interferes with the natural course of mental life. The act of observing one’s own mind changes what is observed. He also criticizes testimony and interpretation of one’s own products as insufficient foundations, because both depend on prior introspective assumptions.

This is important for the philosophy of mind: Herbart is not a Cartesian introspectionist. He does not think the mind is known primarily by clear inner inspection. Mental science requires theory, construction, and indirect explanation.

4. The soul: simple, incorporeal, unknowable, and without innate faculties

Herbart’s psychology is built on a metaphysical soul, but not a Cartesian soul in the familiar sense. The soul is not a self-conscious inner subject furnished with innate powers. It is a simple real: without parts, without internal multiplicity, without spatial extension, and without original mental contents.

In the Text-book, Herbart says that the soul is a “simple essence,” not merely without parts but without any diversity or multiplicity in its quality; hence it has no space relations. When thought in relation to other essences, it must be located at a mathematical point, but this does not make it spatially extended.

He also denies that the soul has innate faculties, concepts, feelings, desires, forms of perception, forms of thought, laws of willing, or predispositions. It is not a Leibnizian monad already full of perceptions; nor is it a passive Lockean tablet receiving impressions as stamps. It is a simple real that responds to disturbances through self-preservations.

The bare nature of the soul is unknowable. Herbart explicitly says that the soul’s simple nature is totally unknown and will remain so; it is not an object of either speculative or empirical psychology. This is a decisive anti-Cartesian point. The soul is metaphysically postulated as the condition of unified mental life, but it is not transparently given to itself as “I.”

The soul’s basic metaphysical function is self-preservation. When other reals disturb it, the soul does not literally divide or acquire parts. Instead, its acts of self-preservation appear psychologically as presentations. Herbart’s translator explains the point: the soul is an absolutely simple existence without inherent powers or talents, and representations are the elements of psychical life, regarded as acts of the soul’s self-preservation.

The soul is therefore incorporeal, but mental life is not the bare soul. Mental life is the system of presentations produced through the soul’s self-preserving reactions. One can formulate the distinction this way:

The soul is the metaphysical ground of mental life.

Mind is the soul as engaged in representing.

Consciousness is the current totality of effective presentations.

The ego is the represented self formed within that system.

This layered structure is one of Herbart’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind.

5. Presentations as the elements of mental life

Herbart’s central thesis is that mental life consists of presentations and their lawful relations. Presentations are not inert images. They are dynamic tendencies, forces, or acts. They strive to maintain themselves, combine with similar presentations, resist incompatible presentations, sink below consciousness when inhibited, and rise again when aided by related presentations.

Herbart’s Text-book states: “Concepts become forces when they resist one another.” Resistance occurs when opposed presentations encounter one another. The Stanford Encyclopedia gives the same core idea: consciousness is a stream of representations, and these are “self-preservations of the soul.”

The most important processes are inhibition, fusion, complication, reproduction, and apperception.

Inhibition occurs when incompatible presentations cannot coexist with full vividness. They crowd, dim, or suppress one another. A presentation is not destroyed by being inhibited. It loses conscious clarity and becomes a striving tendency below the threshold.

Fusion occurs when similar presentations coalesce or reinforce one another.

Complication occurs when heterogeneous presentations are bound together into a complex, for example when visual, tactile, and motor presentations become part of one object-experience.

Reproduction is the return of a suppressed presentation into consciousness, usually aided by a similar or associated presentation.

Apperception is the assimilation of a new presentation or presentation-mass into an already formed mass.

These processes allow Herbart to explain the main operations traditionally assigned to separate faculties. Memory is not a faculty; it is reproduction. Attention is not a faculty; it is the dominance and assimilative activity of certain presentation-masses. Desire is not a faculty; it is the felt rising and striving of a presentation toward effectiveness. Will is not a primitive power; it is a structured motivational outcome within the representational system.

6. Consciousness, threshold, and the unconscious

Herbart’s most historically important psychological idea is the threshold of consciousness, or limen. A presentation is conscious insofar as it is not completely suppressed. When it rises from complete suppression, it crosses the threshold. Herbart emphasizes that it is important to determine the degree of strength a presentation must have to stand beside stronger presentations at the threshold and begin to enter consciousness when hindrance yields.

This yields a dynamic unconscious. Presentations below consciousness are absent from awareness but not annihilated. They remain in the soul as strivings and can influence the conscious field. Herbart writes that suppressed presentations may be out of consciousness while still effective; those preserved by memory remain in “incessant striving to rise,” even when the current state of consciousness is not affected by them.

This is not Freud’s unconscious, but it is a major predecessor of later dynamic models. Herbart uses the same German word, Verdrängung, for displacement or repression, but his meaning is mechanical: one presentation is driven down by others. It is not primarily a theory of disguised wish-fulfillment, sexuality, censorship, or symbolic meaning. Still, the structural resemblance is clear: mental contents can be unconscious, dynamically repressed, and yet efficacious.

Herbart’s mathematical psychology distinguishes statics and mechanics. Statics concerns equilibrium among presentations at a given point. Mechanics concerns their temporal movement toward equilibrium. In simplified form, he gives an equation in which the amount of suppression approaches but never fully reaches the inhibition sum in finite time. The Stanford Encyclopedia reproduces the equation:

\sigma = S(1-e^{-t})

Here S is the inhibition sum and \sigma is the amount inhibited at time t. The important philosophical point is that mental equilibrium is approached asymptotically; waking mental life is never perfectly still. Herbart says that even in the calmest waking person, presentations remain in a “gentle floating.”

This makes consciousness a graded, competitive, temporally dynamic field. It is not a binary container. Presentations can be vivid, dim, marginal, suppressed, mechanically active, statically at threshold, or fully conscious. Herbart’s theory therefore anticipates later ideas of attention as selection, unconscious activation, and graded accessibility.

7. Memory, association, and reproduction

Herbart’s theory of memory is built from the same mechanics. Once formed, presentations remain in the soul. They may be inhibited, but they are not erased. Recollection occurs when a suppressed presentation is helped above threshold by a related presentation.

Immediate reproduction occurs when a new perception directly revives an older presentation of the same or similar object. Mediate reproduction occurs when a presentation is revived through connected presentations. The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes Herbart’s view: a new perception can push back what opposes an older similar representation, allowing the older one to return; mediate reproduction occurs through representations with which the target representation is connected.

This is not merely association by contiguity. Herbart’s series are structured, graded, and dynamically interconnected. A presentation may belong to many series. When one member of a series rises, it tends to pull connected members with it; if the series is interrupted or blocked, different paths of reproduction emerge. His translator notes that the same idea may form a link in several different series, producing complex association.

The philosophy-of-mind significance is substantial. Herbart gives a non-faculty account of retention, recall, habit, expectation, and associative inference. Mental continuity does not require a memory faculty. It requires persisting presentations organized into reproducible series and masses.

8. Perception, concepts, and judgment

Herbart is neither a naive empiricist nor a Kantian transcendentalist about cognition. All concepts arise from experience, but experience is not passively received. New presentations are immediately shaped by older presentation-masses. Cognition is therefore both sensory and constructive.

He rejects innate categories and innate forms of thought. Concepts are “things that have come to be,” not original endowments of the soul. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that for Herbart there are neither original concepts like Kant’s categories nor faculties of concepts like Kant’s understanding or reason.

He distinguishes logical concepts from psychological concepts. A logical concept is content considered apart from its occurrence in an individual mind. A psychological concept is the actual presentation by which such content is thought at a particular time. This distinction is important because it prevents Herbart from reducing logic to psychology, even while explaining the psychological genesis of actual thinking.

Judgment is also explained genetically. A judgment is not merely a fusion, because in a judgment subject and predicate remain distinguishable. Herbart explains judgment through a dynamic fluctuation between a presentation to be determined and older presentations that determine it. His Text-book says that when someone sees a new object, many older concepts are partially reproduced because of similarity; the new concept fluctuates among the old concepts that supply determining characteristics, and the question arises: “What is this object?”

This makes cognition a dynamic act of determination. The mind does not simply receive labeled objects; it stabilizes them by bringing new presentations under older masses.

9. Apperception: the master concept

Apperception is the organizing center of Herbart’s psychology. It is the process by which a new presentation or mass is assimilated into a pre-existing mass. The older mass does not merely receive the new content; it selects, reinforces, modifies, and sometimes suppresses it. The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes Herbart’s famous doctrine: representations form series and stable groupings called representational masses, and apperception is the assimilation of one representation or mass into another.

In outer apperception, sense-given presentations are incorporated into a pre-existing system. Similar elements fuse; incompatible elements are inhibited. The apperceiving mass keeps what is kindred to itself and represses what is antagonistic.

In inner apperception, one presentation-mass becomes the observer of another. This is Herbart’s naturalistic account of self-perception. A stronger and more stable mass can hold, fix, or govern a weaker internal series, making it an object “like” an external object.

Apperception also explains attention. Something can be in consciousness without being the object of attention. Herbart’s commentators give the example of a background hum: it is present in the total conscious state but becomes noticed only when an apperceiving mass fixes it as an object. Genuine perception therefore depends on apperception; to perceive something fully is to perceive it as something, in relation to a concept or mass already formed.

This is one of Herbart’s most modern-sounding ideas. It anticipates top-down processing, schema theory, cognitive assimilation, and the idea that perception is shaped by prior organization. It also explains why education matters. Instruction builds apperception masses. The more articulated the learner’s conceptual masses, the more the learner can perceive, understand, and evaluate. The Stanford Encyclopedia connects apperception directly with education: educators shape concept- and maxim-masses, and well-formed masses allow the skilled musician or physician to perceive what the novice misses.

10. Feeling, desire, affect, passion, and will

Herbart’s treatment of affect and volition is a strict extension of his representational dynamics. Feelings and desires are not separate mental substances, powers, or faculties. They arise from the states and movements of presentations.

The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes Herbart’s thesis: feeling and desire are, in the first place, states of representations. When we feel, something is represented; when we desire, the desired object is represented. The Text-book makes the same point: feelings and desires do not arise from conception in general but from particular concepts, and many different feelings and desires can coexist, agree, or conflict.

Feeling is the consciousness of the way representing is going. If a presentation is compressed, impeded, aided, accelerated, or held back, that dynamic condition is felt. Unpleasantness is often the felt compression of a presentation between opposing forces. Pleasure is not a primitive faculty but a mode of representational movement or release.

Desire is the consciousness of a presentation rising toward greater effectiveness. A desired object is not external to representation; it is the object as presented, striving toward realization. The Stanford Encyclopedia states that desire is the consciousness of a representation’s rising struggle toward brighter light and its tension as it works over obstacles.

Will is not a metaphysically free power standing outside the causal order. Herbart intentionally orders the analysis as representation, action, desire, will. Bodily movement becomes connected with presentations and movement-feelings. When a presentation is excited, it may reproduce the associated feeling and movement unless hindered. Children and animals often pass more directly from presentation to movement; mature human beings are restrained by other concept-masses.

Self-control occurs when maxims or moral judgment-masses inhibit or redirect desire. A desire may project plans, but if the proposed means is felt as morally perverse, moral judgment checks the course of presentations. The eventual outcome depends on whether the moral judgment is connected with a sufficiently strong thought-mass to suppress the growing desire.

This makes Herbart a psychological determinist in one sense: mental events unfold by laws of presentation. But he is not a crude fatalist. Character formation matters because education builds the stable masses that later govern action. Freedom is not an uncaused faculty; it is achieved order, self-command, and harmony between desire and morally formed apperception.

11. The ego: not a substance, but an apperceptive formation

Herbart’s account of the self is one of his most philosophically significant doctrines. He denies that the ego is a metaphysical substance directly known through intellectual intuition. The ego is a psychological phenomenon, but not a primitive one.

He asks: what could self-consciousness be if not an ego turning back on itself? His answer is that the substantial “I” is an illusion generated by relations among presentation-masses. Still, the word “I” refers to a real psychological formation. The ego is not nothing; it is not the soul; it is the represented self within the system of presentations.

The genesis of the ego is developmental. The child first has bodily sensations, desires, and aversions. It then recognizes other beings as centers of representation and action. Only when such recursive phenomena are apperceived within its own body does the representation of its own self arise. The body becomes the gathering place of representations and the origin of movements connected with bodily feelings.

Memory deepens self-consciousness. Through reminiscence, one distinguishes past psychic conditions from present ones and comes to see individual states as accidental to a persisting center. The ego emerges as a representational complex from which many particular components can be abstracted away, making it seem as though the “I” stands independently of them.

Philosophical reflection then corrects this illusion. The ego cannot be thought after subtracting all particular and individual content. Herbart says the ego is a point representable only insofar as innumerable representational sequences point back to it as their common presupposition.

This gives Herbart a powerful non-reductionist, non-Cartesian account of selfhood. The soul is the metaphysical ground of representations. The ego is the represented self formed by the apperceptive organization of those representations. The person is not a bare ego but a formed character: a system of memories, maxims, interests, bodily self-reference, and moral orientation.

In contemporary terms, Herbart’s ego is close to a self-model or narrative-organizational node, though grounded in a metaphysical soul rather than in neural computation.

12. Mind and body

Herbart is not a materialist, but he does not ignore embodiment. The soul is non-extended, but during embodied life it is connected with the nervous system. He even discusses the “seat” of the soul, while cautioning that one can indicate only a region physiologically and not a precise point.

He describes the mind as “the soul endowed with power of representation” and says that the soul’s self-preservations correspond to self-preservations in other essences, including the nervous system. The chain of related internal states may run through a whole bodily system.

Yet Herbart also insists that physiology is not sufficient to explain mental activity. The dependence of mind on body is real, but psychological explanation mainly proceeds through the laws of presentations. He says the nervous system usually appears almost entirely made to obey the mind, though in illness or dementia this relation can be reversed.

The resulting view is best called psychophysical dualism within a pluralist metaphysics of simple reals. The body and soul are not identical. The soul is not extended. But embodied mental life depends on bodily conditions, sensory disturbances, movement-feelings, and nervous mediation.

This is not Cartesian interactionism in the familiar form, because the soul is not a thinking ego with faculties. Nor is it Leibnizian pre-established harmony, because Herbart invokes disturbance, resistance, and self-preservation. Nor is it modern physicalism, because the soul is an incorporeal metaphysical ground.

13. Animals, multiple souls, and the limits of life

Herbart’s rational psychology includes speculative remarks on animal souls and the relation between life and soul. He says there is no reason to assume that the seat of the soul is the same in all animals and humans; in lower animals it may lie in the spinal cord. He even says we cannot assume each brute has only one soul, since severed worm-parts may continue to live.

He rejects the idea of multiple souls in a human being as highly improbable. If reason and passion make it seem as though a person has several souls, that is a psychological phenomenon to be explained by conflict among presentations, not by multiplying souls.

He also distinguishes life from soul. Organic life may continue for some time in separated parts without soul. He speculates, in language that is historically significant but ethically and scientifically obsolete, that some profoundly impaired bodies might be “vegetating bodies without souls.”

These passages matter because they show that Herbart does not identify soul with mere organic vitality. A soul is not simply life. Nor is a mind simply a nervous system. Mentality requires a simple soul capable of representation.

14. Incorporeal soul, immortality, and post-mortem mind

Herbart’s remarks on immortality are not marginal to his system. If the soul is simple, non-extended, and not identical with the body, then bodily dissolution does not by itself destroy the soul. He states directly that the soul is immortal and that the individual’s career cannot be confined to earthly life.

His afterlife theory is strikingly continuous with his psychology. Death removes bodily influences and obstructions. The oldest and strongest concepts regain force. The soul, freed from the body, enters a more complete wakefulness than in life.

Post-mortem existence is not primarily described as sensory experience in another world. It is a reorganization of presentations. Concepts strive toward equilibrium. Different souls undergo different degrees of rearrangement depending on the character of their earthly lives. A peaceful conscience approaches equilibrium more gently; a restless, passion-bound mind undergoes more violent transformations.

Herbart’s final image is almost mathematical-mystical: eternal life is an infinitely gentle fluctuation of concepts, a faint trace of what we call life. The soul, in clear wakefulness, knows and feels the nobility or lack of nobility of its former earthly career, carrying its character as an inseparable good or evil.

This is a distinctive incorporeal philosophy of mind. The soul survives, but what survives psychologically is not an unchanging Cartesian ego. It is the organized system of presentations and character formed in life, now freed from bodily conditions and tending toward equilibrium.

15. Reconstructing Herbart’s philosophy of mind as a systematic doctrine

Herbart’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a set of theses.

The first is the simplicity thesis. Mental life requires a simple metaphysical ground, the soul. This soul is non-extended, without parts, without innate faculties, and unknowable in its own nature.

The second is the anti-faculty thesis. Memory, imagination, understanding, feeling, desire, and will are not explanatory powers. They are names for patterns in the dynamics of presentations.

The third is the presentation thesis. All actual mental life is constituted by presentations or by states, relations, and movements of presentations.

The fourth is the dynamical thesis. Presentations behave like forces: they resist, inhibit, fuse, support, reproduce, and organize one another.

The fifth is the threshold thesis. Consciousness is graded and limited. Presentations may be conscious, marginal, suppressed, or mechanically active below consciousness.

The sixth is the unconscious-efficacy thesis. Suppressed presentations are not destroyed. They continue to strive and can influence later consciousness and action.

The seventh is the apperception thesis. Perception, attention, understanding, learning, and self-awareness depend on the assimilation of new or weaker presentations into older, stronger presentation-masses.

The eighth is the constructivist self thesis. The ego is not the metaphysical soul but an empirical, developmental, apperceptive formation organized around body, memory, action, social recognition, and character.

The ninth is the affective-conative reduction thesis. Feelings, desires, passions, and volitions are states and movements of presentations, not separate faculties.

The tenth is the psychophysical thesis. Mental life is embodied and mediated by the nervous system, but its proper psychological explanation is not reducible to physiology.

The eleventh is the moral-educational thesis. Character is formed by stable masses of interest, judgment, and maxim. Education forms the mind by forming the apperception masses that later govern perception and action.

The twelfth is the immortality thesis. The simple soul survives bodily dissolution, and post-mortem mental life is the continued reorganization of presentations toward equilibrium.

These theses produce a philosophy of mind that is neither materialist, Cartesian, Kantian, nor idealist in any simple sense. It is a metaphysical dualism of soul and body, combined with a naturalistic and formal dynamics of mental representation.

16. Relation to modern philosophy of mind

Herbart is not a physicalist. The mind is not the brain, and the soul is not a physical object. He therefore cannot be assimilated to identity theory, reductive materialism, or eliminativism.

He is not a standard Cartesian dualist either. The soul is not immediately self-known, not furnished with innate faculties, not identical with the ego, and not the transparent subject of introspection. The “I” is a constructed representational object within mental life.

He is not a Kantian transcendental idealist. He rejects innate forms and categories as psychological explanations, and he treats space, time, concepts, and self-consciousness as products of representational development.

He is not a simple associationist. He shares with associationism the idea that mental contents connect and reproduce one another, but his system is much more dynamic. Presentations compete, suppress, fuse, and form structured masses. Prior organization actively shapes new perception.

He is closest to a dynamical representationalism grounded in metaphysical soul-realism. If one bracketed the soul-metaphysics, Herbart would look like a forerunner of theories in which mental states are activation patterns, conscious access is threshold-governed, memory is latent but reactivatable, attention is competitive selection, and selfhood is a constructed model. But Herbart himself would not accept a purely physical or computational replacement of the soul.

His theory of consciousness anticipates graded access theories more than Cartesian inner observation. His theory of unconscious presentations anticipates dynamic unconscious processing, though without Freud’s symbolic and clinical apparatus. His apperception theory anticipates schema-based perception and top-down cognition. His self-theory anticipates developmental and constructivist theories of personal identity. His mathematical psychology anticipates formal modeling, but its actual equations lacked the empirical measurement practices needed for a mature science.

17. Historical position in the development of philosophy of mind

Herbart’s place in the history of philosophy of mind is transitional and unusually important.

Before Herbart, much philosophical psychology was organized around faculties, rational soul theory, or transcendental conditions. Herbart attacks all three in their traditional forms. He replaces faculties with laws of presentation, rational soul-knowledge with an unknowable simple ground, and transcendental ego with a developmental ego-formation.

Against Kant, he tries to show that psychology can be scientific and mathematical. Against Fichte, he denies that the ego is the first principle. Against Hegelian speculative idealism, he insists on plural reals, empirical discipline, and exact psychological explanation.

After Herbart, many of his specific doctrines were transformed rather than preserved intact. His mathematical psychology was criticized, especially once experimental psychology developed more rigorous methods. Huemer and Landerer contrast Herbart and Brentano: Herbart starts from metaphysical principles and mathematizes psychology, emphasizes unconscious repressed presentations, and denies a role for psychological experiments; Brentano rejects metaphysics, bases psychology on inner perception, denies unconscious mental phenomena, and encourages laboratory work through students such as Stumpf and Meinong.

Still, Herbart’s influence was broad. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that his mathematization encouraged Fechner to apply mathematics to the psyche; his account of space influenced Helmholtz and Natorp; Wundt took over his anti-faculty arguments; and his limen and subconscious were important for Freud. Landerer and Huemer also argue that Freud encountered Herbartian ideas through Lindner’s textbook and found there the notion of Verdrängung—repression or displacement—embedded in a mechanistic theory of mind.

Herbart was especially important in Central European and Austrian intellectual life. Huemer and Landerer note that his and Brentano’s systems were two influential attempts to establish psychology as an independent scientific discipline in Central Europe, and they report that up to 1884 all psychology textbooks used in Austro-Hungarian high schools were written in line with Herbartian doctrines.

He also matters for phenomenology. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that Herbart anticipated key ideas later developed in phenomenology, including Brentano’s conception of mental phenomena, and that Herbartian notions of representational force, limen, and reproduction were positively developed by Husserl. This influence should not be overstated as a simple direct lineage, but Herbart belongs to the background against which Brentano, Husserl, Meinong, Twardowski, and other Central European philosophers of mind worked.

18. Strengths and limitations

Herbart’s great strength is explanatory unification. He tries to explain perception, memory, attention, feeling, desire, will, self-consciousness, education, and character through one set of dynamic principles. He avoids the pseudo-explanatory multiplication of faculties and offers a genuinely process-oriented psychology.

His second strength is the threshold model of consciousness. The idea that mental contents can be subthreshold, dynamically active, and later reproduced is one of his most durable insights.

His third strength is the theory of apperception. Herbart sees that perception is not passive reception; it is assimilation by organized prior content. This insight remains central to cognitive science, hermeneutics, pedagogy, and phenomenology.

His fourth strength is the distinction between soul and ego. This allows him to retain metaphysical realism about the soul while denying the naive view that the self as experienced is a simple substance. That distinction makes his view more subtle than many forms of both rational psychology and Cartesian dualism.

His limitations are equally significant. His mathematics is speculative because he lacks reliable methods for measuring the magnitudes he posits. His soul-metaphysics is not empirically testable. His mind-body theory remains obscure by modern standards. His rejection of experimental psychology limited the scientific development of his own program. Some of his claims about animals, impairment, and soul-possession are historically important but scientifically and ethically untenable today. His mechanical language also risks treating meanings as mere forces, leaving underdeveloped the semantic, intentional, and normative dimensions of mental life.

Final interpretation

Herbart’s philosophy of mind can be summarized in one sentence: the mind is the law-governed, dynamically organized life of presentations arising from the self-preservations of a simple incorporeal soul, forming consciousness through thresholds, cognition through apperception, agency through desire and maxim, and selfhood through the developmental organization of representational masses.

The deepest tension in his system is between metaphysics and naturalization. He wants psychology to be a science like physics, but he grounds it in a non-empirical soul. He rejects faculties as mythological, yet he postulates a simple soul that cannot itself be observed. He anticipates modern dynamic and cognitive theories, yet embeds them in a metaphysics of immortal incorporeal beings.

That tension is not a defect to be ignored; it is precisely what makes Herbart historically important. He stands at the point where philosophy of mind begins to move from the metaphysics of the soul toward a science of mental processes, while still refusing to surrender the soul as the ultimate ground of mental unity.

PreviousJames Mill NextThomas Brown

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
